testimonies.statement: Those nearby began to hear a low droning noise, described by one resident as the sound of a jet that never lands.
The organization Earthjustice states that the volume of the machinery in crypto mining facilities can reach 95 decibels, about the same as a moving subway train.

Residents living near mining facilities have reported problems like blood pressure spikes, tinnitus, migraines, permanent hearing loss; and even problems with pets, like hair loss and the development of chronic anxiety in dogs.
In the small village of Dresden, NY, a subdued but constant industrial roar can be heard from the neighborhood streets.

Residents in the Finger Lakes report worsening air quality and constant industrial noise. Tourists who once flocked for peace, wine, and pristine waters now see cooling towers and hear the hum of Bitcoin rigs.
Communities across the country are dealing with the same problems — noise, pollution, water usage — and it's time for a national conversation about the true cost of crypto mining.
They told us the noise wouldn't be a problem. It's a problem. The constant hum keeps us up at night. And now they're expanding again.
The sound is different all the time. Last Tuesday morning it was extremely loud, especially at 5 a.m. in the morning … Sometimes we don't hear it at all and sometimes it's excessively loud.

I've lived in my house for 35 years. So I was there when the power plant was running at full bore and it was at night, not as bothersome as it is now. I think part of the problem is it's steady now. It's not peak and valley noise.
The noise is not abating and more people from further away are hearing it and are being disturbed by the noise.

It's hit and miss. (Some of) the people who live near it say they don't hear anything. One person down the road from there says they hear it all the time. The other one, a mile or so south of there, says they they hear it all the time at their house, but then their neighbors don't hear it.

Add Yahoo on Google BARKER — Residents in Somerset are once again raising concerns about noise at the Lake Mariner Data Center. Those concerns have been heightened with the Somerset town and planning boards approving a proposed site plan from the company that outlines the construction of four additional buildings to house additional bitcoin mining facilities.

Those concerns have been heightened with the Somerset town and planning boards approving a proposed site plan from the company that outlines the construction of four additional buildings to house additional bitcoin mining facilities. Resident Betty Wolanyk claims that the noise has gotten louder since the last two buildings were erected over the past year. Wolanyk was one of about half a dozen residents to speak out on the issue during last week’s town board meeting.

According to town Supervisor Jeff Dewart, crews also installed devices to somewhat mute the roughly 95 decibels that the fans generate. Advertisement Advertisement Dewart said that there isn’t much action officials can take until a proper noise study from the town is completed. Previously studies conducted by Terawulf were deemed inconclusive.

I get four hours of sleep, maybe, because of that constant noise. I can hear the noise even through the storm windows.
It sounds like a 747 jet. It's the loudest in the morning, at night, and if there's high humidity and a breeze.
It's very mentally daunting. It's like having a toothache for 24 hours a day every day.
I'm going to be protesting till the hum is gone, basically, till I get the roar of the falls back because that's what I used to hear.
The noise pollution of this industry is like nothing else that has been there.

Blockfusion spent $25 million to convert a decommissioned coal power plant on Frontier Avenue into a cryptocurrency mining operation. The site became a source of concern for some neighbors who complained about the noise created during the process of Bitcoin mining. City officials later adopted a series of zoning code amendments that imposed strict new regulations on the operation of so-called “high-energy use industries.” Blockfusion’s Frontier Avenue operation shut down in November 2022 after the city notified the company that it was in violation of high-energy use industry amendments, prompting the company to immediately file an application for an operating permit that included changes to the operation in an effort to comply with the new regulations.

"It's to protect the town from the issues that the cities of Niagara Falls and North Tonawanda have been having with cryptocurrency (mining) operators," town Supervisor Sylvia Virtuoso said. Advertisement Advertisement The supervisor said the town was looking to be "proactive in protecting town residents" from common complaints about high-energy use industries such as air and noise pollution. Deputy Town Supervisor Marc Carpenter said, "We watched the struggles of Niagara Falls and North Tonawanda (after high-energy use industries) began operating there.

"Things here are moving quickly and we need to decide if (high-energy use industries) are a good fit for the town and our residents. Not every business is a good fit." After licensing its first crypto-mining facility in 2023, Niagara Falls remains embroiled in an ongoing dispute with a second company over noise concerns. That facility continues to be shut down.

19—TOWN OF NIAGARA — Members of the town board, without debate, moved unanimously Tuesday night to approve a one-year moratorium on "cryptocurrency mining, data processing centers and battery energy storage systems in the town." A public hearing on the proposed moratorium, that proceeded the town board meeting failed to attract any speakers either for or against the proposal. "It's to protect the town from the issues that the cities of Niagara Falls and North Tonawanda have been having with cryptocurrency (mining) operators," town Supervisor Sylvia Virtuoso said. Advertisement Advertisement The supervisor said the town was looking to be "proactive in protecting town residents" from common complaints about high-energy use industries such as air and noise pollution.

Deputy Town Supervisor Marc Carpenter said, "We watched the struggles of Niagara Falls and North Tonawanda (after high-energy use industries) began operating there. We need to take a look at our (zoning) codes and make sure we're prepared." The town board also approved the hiring of a consultant to assist in a review and possible rewriting of the entire town zoning code.

"It might be OK if it's in the right spot," board member Charles Teixeira said. "Things here are moving quickly and we need to decide if (high-energy use industries) are a good fit for the town and our residents. Not every business is a good fit." The updated PUD Petition answers all questions raised by counsel to the City of Niagara Falls related to the completeness of an earlier submission and provides critical new noise data showing the data center will comply with applicable standards. Niagara Falls Redevelopment has partnered with Urbacon Data Centre Solutions, a Toronto-based developer, on the project, and officials believe that working cooperatively to make the Niagara Digital Campus a reality would be a game-changer for the city, its residents, and the local economy.

The first document filed is an updated planned unit development (PUD) Petition, pursuant to Section 1318 of the City Zoning Ordinance. The document answers all questions raised by counsel to the City of Niagara Falls related to the completeness of an earlier, 500-page PUD submission filed in October 2024 and provides critical new noise data showing the data center will comply with the applicable standards. The second filing is a “Text Amendment Request to Revise the city’s High Energy Use Law,” which was enacted in late 2022.

The proposed data center campus would consist of nine buildings totaling over 1.2 million square feet, with NFR owning the majority of the land needed for the project. Concerns have been raised about noise, over-industrialization, and impacts on tourism and history, with a Planning Board workshop planned to address remaining questions and concerns before a vote in September. See more For more than four hours Wednesday night, Falls residents, lawyers, architects, consultants and engineers working for Niagara Falls Redevelopment (NFR) and members of the city’s Planning Board weighed, in sometimes excruciating detail, the pros and cons of a proposed Negotiated Planned Development District, also known as a Planned Unit District (PUD), for the purpose of developing a proposed $1.5 billion data center campus in the South End.

Only two residents attended a public hearing in Niagara Falls regarding a request to modify the city’s zoning code restrictions on high-energy use industries, with one resident urging the Planning Board not to change the existing ordinance. The NFR affiliate is proposing to eliminate the ban on high-energy use industries outside industrial zones, increase permitted noise levels from high-energy facilities, and allow the city council to approve changes to setback and buffer requirements, arguing that these changes would make the law more flexible. The proposed zoning code changes by NFR would eliminate current high-energy use restrictions, potentially allowing data centers or bitcoin mines to be located next to homes in residential neighborhoods, prompting concerns from Planning Board members about protecting residents.

The city council's acceptance of a draft environmental impact statement from BlockFusion triggered a public review process required for the approval of a high-energy use operating permit. Complaints about noise from bitcoin mining facilities led to the city adopting strict regulations on high-energy use industries, causing BlockFusion to cease operations and apply for an operating permit. See more The Niagara Falls Planning Board will hold a public hearing Wednesday on a long delayed request for an operating permit from a Frontier Avenue bitcoin miner.

Creation of the overlay zoning district would allow BlockFusion to obtain a high-energy use industry operating permit from the city and resume its bitcoin mining operations. Almost two years after its future was left in limbo by inaction from the Niagara Falls City Council, city lawmakers in February agreed to accept a draft environmental impact statement (DEIS) from BlockFusion which declares that noise coming from its bitcoin mining operation is within the limits outlined in a series of city zoning ordinances that impose strict regulations on the operation of so-called “high-energy use industries.” Advertisement Advertisement The council’s action triggered the start of a public review process, required for the approval of a high-energy use operating permit for BlockFusion. Adopting the DEIS resolution opened a 31-day window for the public to submit comments on BlockFusion’s permit request.

Adopting the DEIS resolution opened a 31-day window for the public to submit comments on BlockFusion’s permit request. It also re-started a process that stalled in July 2023 when the then-city council members split on approving an operating permit for BlockFusion in a dispute over the amount of noise its bitcoin mining was generating. Complaints about the noise created by bitcoin mining facilities that had set up shop in the Falls led the city, in September 2022, to adopt a series of Zoning Code amendments that imposed strict new regulations on the operation of “high-energy use industries.” At a public hearing on the then-proposed amendments, one Falls resident described the noise coming from a nearby bitcoin mining facility as equivalent to “a 747 revving up on Buffalo Avenue.” The Zoning Code amendments restricted high-energy use facilities like data centers and bitcoin mining operations to locations that are zoned only for industrial uses.

It also re-started a process that stalled in July 2023 when the then-city council members split on approving an operating permit for BlockFusion in a dispute over the amount of noise its bitcoin mining was generating. Complaints about the noise created by bitcoin mining facilities that had set up shop in the Falls led the city, in September 2022, to adopt a series of Zoning Code amendments that imposed strict new regulations on the operation of “high-energy use industries.” At a public hearing on the then-proposed amendments, one Falls resident described the noise coming from a nearby bitcoin mining facility as equivalent to “a 747 revving up on Buffalo Avenue.” The Zoning Code amendments restricted high-energy use facilities like data centers and bitcoin mining operations to locations that are zoned only for industrial uses. The amendments also created an “overlay” to the city’s current industrial zone requirements, adding restrictions that require larger setbacks from residential areas and imposing strict limits on the noise generated by the high energy use operations.

Complaints about the noise created by bitcoin mining facilities that had set up shop in the Falls led the city, in September 2022, to adopt a series of Zoning Code amendments that imposed strict new regulations on the operation of “high-energy use industries.” At a public hearing on the then-proposed amendments, one Falls resident described the noise coming from a nearby bitcoin mining facility as equivalent to “a 747 revving up on Buffalo Avenue.” The Zoning Code amendments restricted high-energy use facilities like data centers and bitcoin mining operations to locations that are zoned only for industrial uses. The amendments also created an “overlay” to the city’s current industrial zone requirements, adding restrictions that require larger setbacks from residential areas and imposing strict limits on the noise generated by the high energy use operations. Advertisement Advertisement When the new requirements went into effect in November 2022, BlockFusion ceased operations and applied for an operating permit for its Frontier Avenue facility.

However, the application languished in a dispute with city officials over the amount of noise coming from the facility and the measures BlockFusion had taken to reduce it. The Zoning Code overlay caps the noise from a high-energy use facility at 65 decibels, measured at the property line of the residence closest to the operation. A BlockFusion attorney has said a consultant, hired by the bitcoin miner, measured the noise level at the nearest residence to their facility, which is surrounded by other industrial operations, at 55.5 decibels.

The Zoning Code overlay caps the noise from a high-energy use facility at 65 decibels, measured at the property line of the residence closest to the operation. A BlockFusion attorney has said a consultant, hired by the bitcoin miner, measured the noise level at the nearest residence to their facility, which is surrounded by other industrial operations, at 55.5 decibels. The city disputed that finding, claiming that when the facility fired up, its operations increased the ambient noise level in its neighborhood above the 65-decibel threshold.

A BlockFusion attorney has said a consultant, hired by the bitcoin miner, measured the noise level at the nearest residence to their facility, which is surrounded by other industrial operations, at 55.5 decibels. The city disputed that finding, claiming that when the facility fired up, its operations increased the ambient noise level in its neighborhood above the 65-decibel threshold. Advertisement Advertisement The adopted council resolution resolves the decibel debate.

The Niagara County Planning Board denied a recommendation for proposed changes to Niagara Falls’ zoning code requirements for high-energy use industries, including bitcoin mines and data centers, by a vote of 5-1. The proposed “text amendment” by Eleventh Street Properties LLC, an affiliate of Niagara Falls Redevelopment, aimed to eliminate a ban on high-energy use industries outside of industrial zones, increase permitted noise levels, and grant the city council the ability to approve waivers to setback and buffer requirements. The changes in high-energy use restrictions sought by NFR are connected to a proposed $1.5 billion data center campus development in Niagara Falls’ South End, with the city council expressing opposition to the proposed amendments.
